Title: An Adaptive Regime-Based Framework for Modeling and Forecasting Non-Stationary Retail Sales Data
Description: This project addresses the fundamental flaw in traditional retail analytics: the assumption of "local stability" in fixed rolling windows. By recognizing that retail environments are dynamic and prone to structural changes—such as promotions, holidays, and global shocks—this research moves beyond simple data smoothing. The framework identifies distinct "regimes" where sales behavior is statistically stable, allowing for models that are not only more accurate in their predictions but also deeply interpretable regarding how specific factors (covariates) influence sales during different market states.
Keywords: Retail Analytics , Non-Stationary Time Series , Regime Detection , Structural Breaks , Adaptive Rolling Windows , Model Interpretability
Methodologies: Partitioning time series into contiguous and data-driven windows , defining regimes as statistically stable time intervals , identifying structural changes through shifts in mean and variability , utilizing a regime detection engine based on the z-score technique , applying a sensitivity threshold ($\zeta$) to declare regime breaks , ensuring a minimum window size ($L_{min}$) for statistical reliability , employing chronological splitting within each regime to prevent data leakage , fitting local regression models to capture regime-specific covariate effects , extracting sign and magnitude of coefficients for local interpretability , calculating Within-Regime Variance Ratio (WRV) to assess segmentation efficiency , using Inter-Regime Mean Separation (IMS) to validate structural breaks , and monitoring the Coefficient Stability Index (CSI) to distinguish between global and regime-specific drivers
Tools Used: Python, Statistical Techniques, Evaluation Metrics, Matplotlib & Seaborn
